imageDescription: Person wrapped in white cloth with arms crossed near chest, a gesture/pose illustration
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person in white wrap making a hand sign near the waist
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person in white wrap with hand near waist, an egg-like object visible at edge of frame
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person making a hand sign near the ear/cheek
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person with both index fingers pointing toward cheeks, smiling
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person with index finger raised near chin in white wrap, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person in white wrap, portrait/pose for sign demonstration
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person extending hand outward with arm gesture, sign demonstration
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person with hand moving downward/forward in front of body, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. Acholi kinship terms vary by maternal/paternal side; 'nero' (maternal uncle) used as a general gloss.

imageDescription: Person with finger pointing/circling near wrist, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Mego' (mother's sister/auntie) used as general gloss for aunt.

imageDescription: Person with both hands raised, palms up, gesturing outward
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Omera' is a general Acholi term for a same-generation relative/cousin.

imageDescription: Person making a hand gesture with arm extended forward, white wrap clothing
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Latin omera' (child of my relative) is a descriptive phrase; Acholi has no single common word for nephew.

imageDescription: Person with both hands open/curved in front of body, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. Descriptive phrase 'child of my relative (girl)' used; no single common Acholi word for niece.

imageDescription: Person with both fists positioned at waist, arms gesturing
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Cwara' (my husband) is the common possessive form used in everyday Acholi speech.

imageDescription: Portrait of a woman in white wrap, sign demonstration pose
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped portrait consistent with other gesture-frame illustrations in this set. 'Dakona' (my wife) is the common possessive form.

imageDescription: Man with hand raised near shoulder, sign gesture, arrow showing motion
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Lunyodo' is the standard Acholi word for parents.

imageDescription: Man making a fist/closed-hand gesture near chest, arrow showing motion toward chest
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Man with both hands raised near chest/shoulders, fingers extended, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration (note: depicted figure appears male, but label refers to the sign itself, which is standard for USL instructional illustrations).

imageDescription: Forearm and clenched fist raised, arrow showing upward motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Latin laco' (male child) is the standard Acholi term for boy.

imageDescription: Man with hand raised near forehead/temple, finger gesture, arrow showing motion
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Latin nyako' (female child) is the standard Acholi term for girl.

imageDescription: Person in profile with hand near hip/waist, pointing gesture, sign demonstration
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Man with both hands curled near chest holding an invisible object, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Nyathi' is the standard Acholi word for baby/infant.

imageDescription: Man pointing with index finger toward shoulder/chest, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Ladit' (elder/grown person) used as gloss for adult.

imageDescription: Multi-figure office/meeting scene: four people seated around a table with laptops, a flip chart with bar graphs, large windows - a colorful illustration scene, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Person' but the image is a multi-person office/meeting illustration (textbook scene furniture), not a USL gesture for 'person'. No single concrete sign can be salvaged from this image; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Forearm with hand making a gesture near the wrist, arrows indicating circular motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: image is a clear Type A gesture illustration with arrows showing motion, and 'Marriage' is a concrete enough vocabulary item to teach as a sign.

imageDescription: Forearm and hand with fingers curled, arrow showing downward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrow. Same Acholi root 'nyom' (marriage/wedding) used as for Marriage entry.

imageDescription: Forearm and closed fist, arrow showing downward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrow, consistent with neighboring marriage-related signs. Acholi has no single word for 'single (unmarried)'; descriptive phrase 'pud peke i nyom' (not yet married) used.

imageDescription: Cropped torso/shoulder of person in white wrap, partial gesture frame
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: cropped portrait/gesture frame consistent in style with other approved gesture illustrations in this set (e.g. family_26_199 Wife). Descriptive Acholi phrase 'oryeko nyom' (has divorced/separated marriage) used as best-effort gloss.

imageDescription: Forearm and hand with fingers curled, arrow showing downward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrow, consistent with neighboring marriage-related signs. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'dako ma cware oto' (woman whose husband has died) used; no single common word.

imageDescription: Man wearing a white skull cap, portrait/pose for sign demonstration
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: portrait/pose consistent in style with other gesture-frame illustrations in this set. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'laco ma dakone oto' (man whose wife has died) used; no single common word.

imageDescription: Person with both fists raised near shoulders, arrows showing upward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrows. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'yubu nyom' (arranging/preparing marriage) used for engagement; no single common word.

imageDescription: Man with both hands extended, fingers pointing downward, arrows showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrows. 'Wat' (kinship/relation/bond) used as natural Acholi gloss.

imageDescription: Hand with palm flat against chest, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Mar' is the common Acholi word for love.

imageDescription: Hand with fingers curled near chest pocket, sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Gwoko' (to keep/care for/protect) used as gloss.

imageDescription: Hand with fingers extended near chest/shoulder, sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'tic ma omiyo' (duty/work given to one) used; no single common word for the abstract concept of 'responsibility'.

imageDescription: Forearm and hand making a circular gesture, arrow showing rotational motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Person in white wrap with both hands gesturing in front of chest (similar framing to other family-section gestures)
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: gesture-frame illustration consistent with style of other approved signs in this set. 'Ot pa kaka' (house of the clan/family) used to distinguish from plain 'Home'/'House'.

imageDescription: Man with both hands raised near shoulders, fists clenched, arrows showing upward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrows. 'Kaka' (clan/lineage) used as natural Acholi gloss for ancestry.

imageDescription: Man with both hands sweeping outward from chest, palms forward, smiling - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Man with both hands curled near eyes, fingers spread, arrows showing upward motion near face - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Man with both hands held in front, palms up, fingers curling toward self - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Man with both hands raised near temples, fingers curled, arrows showing upward motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration.

imageDescription: Man with hand raised palm-out near face/chin, arm extended outward - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Bwoyo' (to wonder/be amazed) used as Acholi gloss for surprised.

imageDescription: Man with both hands held loosely in front of chest, wavy motion lines suggesting limp/idle gesture - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. Corrected acholiLabel: previous value 'makwar' actually means 'red' in the glossary, not 'bored' - replaced with descriptive phrase 'cwiny pe yom' (heart/mind not pleased = bored).

imageDescription: Man with closed fist near chin, arrow showing motion away from face - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Yom cwiny' (joy/lightness of heart) used as Acholi gloss for excited.

imageDescription: Man with both hands held in front at chest level, fingers spread, wavy arrows showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'tam obale' (thoughts are mixed up) used for confused.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and hand near hip, sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. Corrected acholiLabel: previous value 'makwar' actually means 'red' in the glossary, not 'tired' - replaced with 'ol' (to be tired), the common Acholi word.

imageDescription: Man with both hands held near stomach, circular motion arrow - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Kec' is the standard Acholi word for hunger.

imageDescription: Man with hand near throat/neck, fingers pointing down, arrow showing downward motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Riyo' is the standard Acholi word for thirst.

imageDescription: Both hands held in front of chest with fingers spread, arrows showing upward motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Tuo' is the standard Acholi word for sickness.

imageDescription: Man wearing a patterned cap, with a thermometer near his mouth, partial-frame illustration
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped portrait/gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. 'Cango' (to heal/get well) used as gloss for 'Better'.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and clenched fist, partial gesture frame
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. 'Mar' is the common Acholi word for love.

imageDescription: Man leaning back with hand on hip, sign gesture/pose
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Kwero' (to refuse/detest) used as gloss for hate.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and hand near hip, partial gesture frame
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. 'Nyeko' is the common Acholi word for jealousy.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and hand near waist, partial gesture frame with motion lines
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. 'Pwoc' (pride/honor) used as gloss for proud.

imageDescription: Man with hand near chin/jaw, looking downward, gesture pose with sign demonstration
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Lewic' is the standard Acholi word for shame.

imageDescription: Portrait of a man, gesture/pose frame consistent with other sign illustrations
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped portrait/gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. 'Bal' (sin/wrongdoing, hence guilt) used as gloss.

imageDescription: Man with hand near mouth/chin, index finger raised, looking up - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Geno' (hope/trust) used as gloss for hopeful.

imageDescription: Woman with both hands held near hips, fists clenched, arrows showing vertical motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Parro' (to worry/think too much) used as gloss for worried.

imageDescription: Woman with both hands held in front of body, fists with extended thumbs, double-headed arrows showing vertical trembling motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration (hands trembling/shaking motion fits 'Nervous'). Descriptive Acholi phrase 'diu diu' (trembling) used for nervous.

imageDescription: Two children watering a young tree/sapling outdoors - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Calm' but the image is a multi-figure outdoor scene (children planting/watering a tree), a different illustration style/section (environmental education) unrelated to the USL gesture illustrations. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Two children watering a planted tree sapling outdoors - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Peaceful' but the image is the same multi-figure outdoor planting scene as emotions_23_156, not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Cartoon figure standing beside a large green bush/tree on a street corner - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Pain' but the image is a street-scene illustration with a person and a bush/tree, from a different section (environmental/community scenes), not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Cartoon couple sitting on a park bench with a dog nearby - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture

imageDescription: Park scene with multiple figures: a seesaw, a phone booth, a pond, people walking - colorful collage illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Stomachache' but the image is a busy park-scene collage (seesaw, phone booth, pond), from a different section, not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Street scene with a car, pedestrians, a crossing, motorcycle, and shops - colorful collage illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Toothache' but the image is a busy street-scene collage (car, pedestrians, crosswalk), from a different section, not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Suburban street scene with a yellow house, large green tree/bush, two figures and a car - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Fever' but the image is a suburban street scene (house, tree, car, pedestrians), from a different section, not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Urban scene with trees between buildings and a child on a bicycle - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Cough' but the image is an urban street scene with trees and a child cycling, from a different section, not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Large purple multi-story building surrounded by trees, with a small figure in front - colorful scene illustration, not a sign gesture
notes: MISLABEL: previously approved as 'Cold/Flu' but the image is a building/school illustration with surrounding trees, from a different section, not a USL gesture. No concrete single sign can be salvaged; rejected as Type C.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and hand with fingers curled near wrist, partial gesture frame
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: cropped gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. 'Yot kom' (body comfort/wellness) used as natural Acholi gloss for health.

imageDescription: Person with hand near chest, fingers spread, arrows indicating motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Yat' is the standard Acholi word for medicine.

imageDescription: Woman holding her wrist/forearm with the other hand, gesture pose, dotted motion lines
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Adagitari'/'Daktari' (loanword) is the commonly used Acholi term for doctor.

imageDescription: Man with both hands raised near chest, fingers curled - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Nasi' (loanword) is the commonly used Acholi term for nurse.

imageDescription: Woman with hand raised, fist near shoulder, arrow showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Ospitale' (loanword) is the commonly used Acholi term for hospital.

imageDescription: Portrait of a person in white wrap, gesture/pose frame consistent with other sign illustrations
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped portrait/gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'kabedo me yat' (place of medicine) used for clinic.

imageDescription: Person with arm extended forward, hand open, palm facing up, arrow showing outward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrow. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'coro yat' (administering medicine) used for treatment.

imageDescription: Person with fist raised near shoulder, arrow showing upward/circular motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrow. 'Cango' (healing/getting well) reused from the 'Better' entry as gloss for recovery.

imageDescription: Person with fist held near chest, arrow showing downward/outward motion - sign gesture
notes: Promoted from hold to approved: clear Type A gesture illustration with motion arrow. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'twero piyo piyo' (matter requiring quick action) used for emergency; no single common word.

imageDescription: Man with raised fist, arrow pointing outward, numbered diagram markers (1,2,3) showing a multi-step gesture sequence
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration (multi-step sign sequence). 'Ambulansi' (loanword) is the commonly used Acholi term for ambulance.

imageDescription: Portrait of a smiling person in white wrap, gesture/pose frame consistent with other sign illustrations
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped portrait/gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'kony makwongo' (first/initial help) used for first aid.

imageDescription: Man with both fists held near waist, gesture pose - sign demonstration
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Gengo' (to prevent/block) used as gloss for prevention.

imageDescription: Cropped portrait of a person looking downward, gesture/pose frame consistent with other sign illustrations
notes: Matches existing approval; cropped portrait/gesture frame consistent in style with other approved illustrations in this set. Descriptive Acholi phrase 'yer kom' (cleanliness of the body) used for hygiene.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and hand making an open-palm sweeping gesture, double-headed arrow showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Leng' is the standard Acholi word for clean.

imageDescription: Cropped forearm and hand with fingers curled near wrist, double-headed arrows showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Dwol' used as gloss for dirty.

imageDescription: Man with raised fist, arm extended upward and outward, arrow showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Sabuni' (widely used loanword) is the standard term for soap.

imageDescription: Woman with arm extended, hand open and palm up, double-headed vertical arrow showing motion - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. Expanded acholiLabel from 'pii' (water) to 'pii ma leng' (clean water) to match the full English label 'Water (Clean)'.

imageDescription: Woman with arm extended forward, hand making a beckoning/patting gesture, circular arrow showing motion near waist - sign gesture
notes: Matches existing approval; clear Type A gesture illustration. 'Gwoko' (protection/safekeeping) reused from the 'Care' entry as gloss for safety.
